skill.name: controlnet-pose
skill.description: Pose-conditioned generation on RunComfy via the `runcomfy` CLI. Routes across Kling 2-6 Motion Control Pro / Standard (transfer the motion / blocking of a reference video onto a target character), community Wan 2-2 Animate (audio-driven character animation with pose conditioning), and Z-Image Turbo ControlNet LoRA (pose-conditioned image generation from an OpenPose / DWPose / canny / depth control image). Picks the right route based on video vs still and stylized vs photoreal. Triggers on "controlnet", "control net", "pose control", "openpose", "DWPose", "transfer pose", "motion control", "pose driven", "character pose", "depth control", "canny edge", "use this pose", or any explicit ask to condition generation on a pose / skeleton / motion / depth / canny reference.
skill.summary: Pose-conditioned AI image and video generation using ControlNet on RunComfy
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: Evaluated all 76 static findings. External command detections are markdown CLI examples (false positives). Network URLs are documentation links. Filesystem reference is legitimate token storage. No cryptographic operations or reconnaissance code exists. All patterns are legitimate skill documentation with appropriate security controls.

content.user_title: Generate AI Images and Videos with Pose Control
content.value_statement: Artists and developers struggle to create consistent character poses in AI generation. This skill enables pose-conditioned generation via ControlNet, transferring motion from reference videos or locking poses from skeleton images.
actual_capabilities: Transfer motion from a reference video to a target character image using Kling 2-6 Motion Control Pro
Generate pose-locked images from OpenPose, DWPose, canny, or depth control images via Z-Image ControlNet LoRA
Route between video pose transfer and image pose conditioning based on user input type
Access ComfyUI workflows for multi-condition ControlNet stacks (depth + canny + pose)
Handle stylized character animation with Wan 2-2 Animate
Configure output directory and poll for generation status
limitations: Does not generate pose skeletons from source images - users must use separate pose estimation tools
Cannot access ComfyUI workflows via CLI - requires RunComfy web interface for GUI workflows
Single-condition pose generation only - multi-condition stacks need ComfyUI
Photoreal face transfer may require additional face-swap pass for identity consistency

use_cases.title: Video Game Concept Art
use_cases.description: Create game character concepts with consistent poses across variations. Upload a pose reference image, then generate multiple character designs in that same stance for sprite sheets and animation planning.

use_cases.title: Motion Choreography Pre-visualization
use_cases.description: Transfer dance or sports movements from reference videos onto stylized characters. Choreographers can preview how complex movements will look in their animated characters before production.

use_cases.title: Product Photography Mockups
use_cases.description: Generate product showcase images with models in specific poses. Fashion brands can create consistent catalog imagery by conditioning on pose references without needing full photoshoots.

prompt_templates.title: Video Motion Transfer (Beginner)
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a dance video at [VIDEO_URL] and a character image at [CHARACTER_URL]. Transfer the motion from the video to the character.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when you have a reference video showing desired movement and a target character image

prompt_templates.title: Pose-Locked Image Generation (Intermediate)
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an image of [PROMPT] using the pose from [POSE_IMAGE_URL] as the control image. Use OpenPose format.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when you have a pose skeleton image and want to create a character in that specific stance

prompt_templates.title: Depth-Controlled Composition (Advanced)
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a cinematic scene of [PROMPT] with the depth map at [DEPTH_URL] controlling the composition. Lock the spatial structure.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when you need precise spatial control over the generated image layout

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Condition Stacked Generation (Advanced)
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ComfyUI workflow for multi-condition ControlNet. Set up depth + canny + pose conditioning to generate [PROMPT] with all three control inputs.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when you need complex control over both pose and edge details

output_examples.input: Transfer the dance moves from https://example.com/dancer.mp4 to the character at https://example.com/knight.png
output_examples.output: Generated video of the knight character performing the dancer's movements with Kling 2-6 Motion Control Pro. The knight's armor and design remain consistent while adopting the reference dance choreography. Output saved to ./out/ directory.

output_examples.input: Create a warrior in a combat stance using the pose skeleton at https://example.com/pose-skeleton.png
output_examples.output: Generated a medieval warrior character in battle stance, matching the OpenPose skeleton provided. The character has traditional armor with a cinematic forest background. Created using Z-Image Turbo ControlNet LoRA with pose conditioning.

output_examples.input: Generate a samurai portrait using the depth map at https://example.com/depth-map.png, prompt: Japanese samurai in cherry blossom garden
output_examples.output: Generated a samurai portrait with the depth map controlling spatial composition. The cherry blossom trees and garden elements are positioned according to the depth map structure, creating a layered cinematic composition.

best_practices: Use clean, single-subject reference videos for motion transfer - avoid scene cuts or multiple performers in the source
Generate control images (OpenPose, DWPose, depth) separately before passing to the skill - this skill does not include pose estimation
Match control image type to the model: OpenPose skeletons for pose control, depth maps for spatial composition, canny for edge guidance
anti_patterns: Do not pass URLs from untrusted sources - the skill treats all provided URLs as user-provided and may process them
Do not expect multi-condition ControlNet stacks from the CLI - route to ComfyUI workflows for complex conditioning
Do not use stylized character references when targeting photoreal output - identity transfer may fail without additional face-swap steps

faq.question: What is the difference between Kling Motion Control and Z-Image ControlNet LoRA?
faq.answer: Kling Motion Control works on video, transferring motion from a reference video to a target character. Z-Image ControlNet LoRA works on still images, using a pose skeleton or depth map as a control image to guide generation.

faq.question: Do I need to generate the pose skeleton myself?
faq.answer: Yes. This skill accepts pose control images (OpenPose, DWPose, canny, depth) but does not generate them. Use separate pose estimation tools like OpenPose or DWPose to create your control image first.

faq.question: Can I use multiple control conditions at once?
faq.answer: The CLI endpoints support single-condition pose control. For multi-condition stacks (pose + depth + canny), use the ComfyUI workflows hosted at runcomfy.com/comfyui-workflows through the web interface.

faq.question: What format should my reference video be in?
faq.answer: Use clean, well-framed videos with a single subject performing one continuous action. Avoid scene cuts, multiple performers, or busy backgrounds. MP4 format is recommended.

faq.question: Why do stylized characters work better than photoreal for motion transfer?
faq.answer: Stylized characters (anime, illustration) maintain consistency when motion is transferred. Photoreal faces may require an additional face-swap pass after motion transfer to preserve identity in the target character.

faq.question: How do I store my RunComfy API token securely?
faq.answer: In development, use `runcomfy login` which stores the token in ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with mode 0600 permissions. In CI environments, set the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable instead.
